title: Trucking finance glossary
description: Operating authority, factoring, and operator-type terms — vocabulary every owner-operator and small fleet uses.

title: MC Number (MC#)
description: Federal operating authority number issued by FMCSA that identifies for-hire interstate motor carriers and brokers.

title: DOT Number (USDOT)
description: USDOT-issued registration number identifying any vehicle subject to federal safety oversight, including private and for-hire carriers.

title: BOC-3
description: FMCSA filing designating process agents in every state where a carrier operates, required to activate operating authority.

title: IRP
description: Reciprocal apportioned-registration agreement among US states and Canadian provinces for commercial vehicles operating across jurisdictions.

title: IFTA
description: Reciprocal fuel-tax agreement among US states and Canadian provinces consolidating fuel-tax reporting for interstate commercial vehicles.

title: UCR
description: Annual federal fee program funding state-level commercial-vehicle enforcement, required for interstate carriers regardless of state of registration.

title: Recourse Factoring
description: Factoring arrangement where the carrier remains liable for unpaid invoices if the broker fails to pay; lower rates than non-recourse.

title: Non-Recourse Factoring
description: Factoring arrangement where the factor absorbs broker insolvency risk on clean deliveries; higher rates than recourse.

title: Owner-Operator
description: Independent trucking professional who owns or leases their truck and operates under their own MC authority or as a subcontractor.

title: Lease-Purchase
description: Carrier-administered program where a driver leases a truck with payments structured to result in eventual ownership; high failure rate.

title: MCS-150
description: Biennial update form filed with FMCSA to refresh a carrier's operational data and keep DOT/MC authority active.

title: CSA Score (CSA)
description: FMCSA Compliance, Safety, Accountability program scoring system that rates carrier safety performance using roadside inspection and crash data.

title: ELD
description: Electronic Logging Device that automatically records driving time, replacing paper logbooks; mandated for most CDL operators since December 2017.

title: FMCSA
description: Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration — DOT agency that regulates commercial motor vehicles, issues operating authority, and enforces safety rules.

title: Hours of Service (HOS)
description: FMCSA rules limiting daily and weekly driving time for commercial drivers, designed to prevent fatigue-related crashes.

title: EIN
description: Employer Identification Number issued by the IRS to identify a business entity for tax purposes; required for most trucking authority filings.

title: Bill of Lading (BOL)
description: Legal document between carrier and shipper that serves as receipt of freight, contract of carriage, and document of title.

title: Proof of Delivery (POD)
description: Signed document confirming the consignee received the freight in acceptable condition; required to factor most trucking invoices.

title: OOIDA
description: Owner-Operator Independent Drivers Association — trade group representing independent owner-operators on regulatory and policy issues.

title: ATA
description: American Trucking Associations — national trade association representing primarily larger fleet operators on regulatory, legislative, and economic policy.

title: CDL Class A (CDL-A)
description: Commercial Driver's License Class A — required for combination vehicles over 26,001 lbs GCWR with a towed unit over 10,000 lbs; the standard CDL for OTR trucking.

title: Hazmat Endorsement (H endorsement)
description: Additional CDL endorsement authorizing the driver to haul hazardous materials in placardable quantities; requires TSA background check.

title: ELDT
description: Entry-Level Driver Training — federal training standard required since February 2022 for new CDL applicants, upgrades, or hazmat endorsements.

title: SCAC
description: Standard Carrier Alpha Code — 2-4 letter unique identifier assigned by NMFTA to motor carriers, brokers, and freight forwarders for EDI and intermodal use.

title: HVUT (Form 2290) (HVUT)
description: Heavy Vehicle Use Tax — annual federal tax on commercial vehicles over 55,000 lbs GVWR, filed via IRS Form 2290; required for IRP registration.

title: Medical Examiner's Certificate (MEDC)
description: DOT-required medical certification verifying a commercial driver's physical fitness to operate; issued by a National Registry medical examiner.

title: Hot-Shot Trucking
description: Time-sensitive freight hauled by light- or medium-duty pickups with goosenecks, typically Class 3–5 trucks running expedited LTL loads.

title: Reefer
description: Refrigerated trailer (or the freight that requires temperature control); standard equipment for hauling produce, frozen goods, and pharma.

title: Dry Van
description: Standard enclosed trailer (53-foot box) for non-perishable, non-temperature-controlled freight; the most common trailer type in trucking.

title: Flatbed
description: Open trailer with no walls or roof, used for oversized, irregularly shaped, or top-loaded freight; requires tarping and load securement skills.

title: Deadhead
description: Empty miles run without revenue freight, typically returning to home base or repositioning for the next load.

title: Over-the-Road (OTR)
description: Long-haul trucking covering significant distances, typically multi-state routes with drivers spending days or weeks away from home.

title: Less-Than-Truckload (LTL)
description: Freight model where carriers consolidate multiple shippers' loads into a single trailer; loads are typically 100–10,000 lbs and below truckload.

title: Full Truckload (FTL)
description: Freight that fills an entire trailer for a single shipper, typically over 10,000 lbs or by volume; the standard model for OTR carriers.

title: Intermodal
description: Freight that travels in containers or trailers across multiple modes (truck + rail + ocean) without the freight itself being unloaded between modes.

title: Drayage
description: Short-distance trucking, typically the first or last leg of an intermodal move, hauling containers between port/rail and warehouses or consignees.

title: Expedited Freight
description: Time-sensitive freight requiring immediate or guaranteed delivery, typically using sprinters, straight trucks, or hot-shot setups for premium rates.

title: Hazmat
description: Hazardous materials freight regulated by DOT under 49 CFR; requires specialized endorsement on CDL plus carrier-level hazmat permitting.

title: Lumper Fee (Lumper)
description: Fee paid for third-party labor that unloads or loads a trailer at a warehouse or dock; common at grocery DCs and large retailers.

title: Truck Order Not Used (TONU)
description: Compensation paid to a carrier when a load is cancelled after the truck is dispatched but before pickup; partial payment for the trip.

title: Detention Pay
description: Compensation paid to a carrier when loading or unloading takes longer than the contractually free time (typically 2 hours).

title: Accessorial Charges
description: Additional fees on a freight bill beyond the base line-haul rate — detention, lumper, layover, fuel surcharge, tolls, etc.

title: Dispatch Fee
description: Percentage of revenue paid to a dispatch service (often 5–10%) for finding loads, negotiating rates, and handling broker relationships.

title: Fuel Surcharge (FSC)
description: Variable line item on a freight bill adjusting compensation for fuel price fluctuations; calculated from the DOE national diesel price benchmark.

title: Demurrage
description: Penalty charged by a steamship line or rail when an intermodal container is held beyond the free time at a port or terminal.

title: Layover
description: Compensation paid when a driver is required to wait overnight or longer at a pickup/delivery beyond normal turnaround time; typically $100–$250/day.

title: Backhaul
description: Return load picked up after delivering the outbound freight, converting an empty deadhead return into revenue.

title: Headhaul
description: The outbound or higher-paying direction in a lane pair; the primary load that drives the lane's economics.

title: Weight Ticket
description: Documented certified weight measurement of a loaded truck from a certified scale (CAT scale, public weigh station, or shipper scale).

title: Dock Fee
description: Fee charged by a warehouse or distribution center for the use of dock space during loading or unloading; sometimes bundled into lumper.

title: Freight Class (NMFC)
description: NMFC-designated freight classification (50–500) that determines LTL pricing based on density, value, fragility, handling difficulty, and stowability.

title: Advance Rate
description: The percentage of an invoice's face value that a factoring company advances to the carrier, typically 80–97%; remainder is held in reserve until broker pays.

title: ACH
description: Automated Clearing House — electronic bank transfer network used for direct deposit of factoring advances and most carrier-to-broker payments.

title: Merchant Cash Advance (MCA)
description: Lump-sum cash advance against future business revenue, typically with daily ACH deductions; high effective APR but easier qualification than term loans.

title: Asset-Based Lending (ABL)
description: Revolving credit facility secured by accounts receivable, equipment, or inventory; typical for fleets with $5M+ annual revenue.

title: Equipment Loan
description: Term loan secured by the financed vehicle (truck, trailer, or other equipment); standard structure for buying Class 8 tractors and trailers.

title: Working Capital
description: Short-term unsecured business funding used to bridge cash-flow gaps, cover operating expenses, or capitalize on opportunities; APR typically 14–34%.

title: Line of Credit
description: Revolving credit facility allowing the carrier to draw funds as needed up to an approved limit; pays interest only on drawn balance.

title: Term Loan
description: Lump-sum business loan repaid over a fixed schedule with interest; the standard structure for equipment purchases and major capital expenditures.

title: SBA Loan (SBA)
description: Small Business Administration-guaranteed loan with longer terms (up to 10–25 years) and lower rates than conventional commercial loans.

title: Lockbox
description: Address or bank account designated for invoice payments where the factoring company receives broker payments directly, used to control collections.

title: UCC-1
description: Uniform Commercial Code financing statement filed by a lender or factor to publicly establish a security interest in business assets.

title: Balloon Payment
description: Large lump-sum payment due at the end of a loan term, with smaller monthly payments throughout the term; common in equipment financing.

title: Cost Per Mile (CPM)
description: Total operating cost divided by total miles driven; the diagnostic metric that defines whether a lane or contract is profitable.

title: Revenue Per Mile (RPM)
description: Total revenue divided by total miles driven; the headline number quoted in spot-market and contract pricing, but only meaningful when compared to CPM.

title: All-In Rate
description: Combined rate per mile or per load that includes line-haul, fuel surcharge, and all accessorials in a single flat number.

title: Broker Spread
description: The difference between what a shipper pays a freight broker and what the broker pays the carrier; the broker's gross margin on the load.

title: Primary Liability
description: Commercial auto insurance covering bodily injury and property damage to others when at fault; FMCSA mandates $750K–$5M minimum based on cargo.

title: Motor Truck Cargo (MTC)
description: Insurance coverage protecting the freight in transit; required by most brokers and shippers, typically $100K minimum for general freight.

title: Physical Damage
description: Coverage on the carrier's own truck and trailer against collision, theft, fire, vandalism, and other damage; typically required by equipment lenders.

title: General Liability (GL)
description: Commercial general liability covering bodily injury and property damage from non-trucking-related business activities (premises, completed operations).

title: Non-Trucking Liability (NTL)
description: Bobtail coverage protecting an owner-operator leased on with a carrier when driving the truck for personal/non-business use.

title: Occupational Accident
description: Coverage replacing workers compensation for owner-operators (1099 contractors) covering injury and disability while working.

title: Reefer Breakdown
description: Insurance endorsement covering cargo loss from refrigeration unit failure; standard motor truck cargo policies exclude this.

title: Premium Financing
description: Loan structured to spread an annual insurance premium into monthly payments; widely used in trucking where premiums often exceed 10% of revenue.

title: Deductible
description: The portion of a covered claim the insured pays before insurance pays; higher deductibles lower the premium but increase out-of-pocket exposure.

title: AM Best
description: Independent rating agency that grades insurance carriers' financial strength; ratings affect which carriers are acceptable to brokers and lenders.

title: DOT Class
description: Federal vehicle classification (Class 1–8) based on gross vehicle weight rating; affects insurance pricing, licensing, and lender treatment.

title: Declarations Page (Dec Page)
description: Summary page of an insurance policy listing covered vehicles, drivers, coverage limits, deductibles, and effective dates; the proof-of-insurance document.

title: Hometime
description: Scheduled time a long-haul driver spends at home between OTR trips; varies by carrier and driver agreement, typically 2-4 days every 2-3 weeks.

title: Slip-Seat
description: Operational model where multiple drivers share the same truck on alternating shifts, maximizing equipment utilization (60+ hours per truck per day).

title: Team Driving
description: Two drivers alternating shifts in the same truck simultaneously, keeping the freight moving 18-20 hours per day; premium lanes pay 50%+ more than solo.

title: Forced Dispatch
description: Carrier policy requiring a driver to accept assigned loads or face disciplinary action; common at large fleets, contentious in lease-on arrangements.

title: Sleeper Berth
description: Compartment behind the cab where a driver sleeps; HOS regulations allow split rest periods (8/2 or 7/3) when berth is properly equipped.

title: DOT Physical
description: Medical examination required for commercial drivers by FMCSA, conducted by certified examiners; validity ranges from 3 months to 24 months based on health.

title: Drug & Alcohol Clearinghouse (Clearinghouse)
description: FMCSA-operated database tracking commercial driver drug and alcohol violations; mandatory query for hiring and annual checks since 2020.

title: Pre-Employment Screening (PSP) (PSP)
description: FMCSA system providing motor carriers a driver's 5-year crash history and 3-year inspection history before hiring decisions.

title: Cents-Per-Mile Pay (CPM)
description: Driver pay model based on a fixed cents-per-mile rate for loaded (and sometimes empty) miles; the dominant pay structure for OTR truck drivers.

title: Percentage-of-Line-Haul (%-of-line-haul)
description: Driver pay model giving a percentage (typically 60-80%) of the freight line-haul rate; common for owner-operators leased on with carriers.

title: Lease-On Driver
description: Owner-operator operating under a carrier's authority via a permanent lease arrangement; receives loads from the carrier and pays a percentage to operate.

title: Company Driver
description: W-2 employee driver operating a carrier-owned truck under the carrier's authority; carrier handles all operating costs and pays the driver per mile or salary.

title: Per Diem
description: Daily meal & incidental expense allowance ($69 in 2026) that truck drivers can deduct from taxable income; 80% deductible for transportation workers.

title: MACRS Depreciation (MACRS)
description: Modified Accelerated Cost Recovery System — IRS depreciation method for business assets; semi trucks depreciate over 3 years on a 200% declining balance.

title: Section 179
description: IRS provision allowing immediate expense deduction of up to $1.16M (2026) on qualifying business assets in the year placed in service.

title: Quarterly Estimated Taxes (1040-ES)
description: Federal estimated tax payments due quarterly (April, June, September, January) for self-employed taxpayers including owner-operators.

title: Self-Employment Tax (SE Tax)
description: 15.3% federal tax on net self-employment income (12.4% Social Security + 2.9% Medicare); paid by sole proprietors, partners, and LLC owner-operators.

title: Schedule C
description: IRS Form 1040 Schedule C — Profit or Loss from Business; used by sole-proprietor owner-operators to report business revenue, expenses, and net profit.

title: Mileage Log
description: Daily record of miles driven, route, purpose, and odometer readings; required for tax deduction support and IFTA reporting.

title: 1099-NEC
description: IRS Form 1099-NEC reports non-employee compensation paid to independent contractors; brokers issue 1099-NEC to carriers who received $600+ in a year.

title: Independent Contractor Classification
description: IRS, DOL, and state-level legal classification of worker as 1099 contractor (vs W-2 employee); test factors vary; misclassification is a major regulatory risk.

title: Fuel Tax Credit
description: Federal tax credit (Form 4136) refunding excise tax on fuel used for off-highway business purposes; minor for most truckers but valuable in niche operations.

title: Actual Expenses vs Standard Mileage
description: IRS allows two methods for deducting vehicle expenses: actual expenses (all costs) or standard mileage rate; truckers typically use actual expenses.

title: Tax Home
description: IRS-defined principal place of business; for truckers, typically the home base or domicile where the driver lives between trips.

title: Dash Cam
description: Forward-facing in-cab camera recording the road; provides video evidence in accidents and is increasingly required by insurance carriers and shippers.

title: AI Dash Cam
description: Dash cam with onboard AI detecting risky driving events (harsh braking, lane departure, distraction), generating real-time alerts and scoring driver behavior.

title: TMS
description: Transportation Management System — software platform for managing trucking operations (dispatch, billing, document management, settlements, compliance).

title: Dispatch Software
description: Software for assigning loads to trucks, tracking shipments, and communicating with drivers; subset of TMS but available as standalone for smaller fleets.

title: Load Board
description: Online marketplace where freight brokers post loads and carriers find freight; major platforms: DAT, Truckstop, 123Loadboard.

title: Geofencing
description: GPS-based virtual perimeter around a location; alerts/automates actions when a truck enters or exits the zone (arrival notification, automatic detention timer).

title: DVIR
description: Driver Vehicle Inspection Report — daily pre-trip and post-trip vehicle inspection record required by FMCSA; logs defects and corrective actions.

title: Vehicle Telematics
description: Connected vehicle data (location, speed, fuel, engine diagnostics, driver behavior) transmitted to fleet management systems for analysis and reporting.

title: Driver Scorecard
description: Telematics-based performance metric scoring driver behavior (harsh braking, speeding, idling) on a 0-100 scale; used for coaching and pay incentives.

title: IFTA Reporting Software
description: Software automating quarterly IFTA fuel-tax-by-state reporting using ELD mileage data and fuel-purchase records; reduces audit risk and prep time.

title: Fleet Management Platform
description: Integrated software combining ELD, telematics, dash cams, fuel cards, and reporting in a single dashboard; standard for fleets of 5+ trucks.

title: Mobile-App ELD
description: ELD device that uses a smartphone/tablet as the user interface (paired with a hardware ECM-connected device); alternative to all-in-one hardware ELDs.

title: Tanker Endorsement (N endorsement)
description: CDL endorsement (N) authorizing the driver to haul liquids or gases in a permanently mounted tank with capacity over 1,000 gallons.

title: Doubles/Triples Endorsement (T endorsement)
description: CDL endorsement (T) authorizing the driver to operate combination vehicles with two or three trailers; common in LTL fleet operations.

title: Passenger Endorsement (P endorsement)
description: CDL endorsement (P) authorizing the driver to transport 16 or more passengers; required for charter buses, transit, and certain shuttle operations.

title: School Bus Endorsement (S endorsement)
description: CDL endorsement (S) authorizing the driver to operate a school bus; requires P endorsement plus additional school-bus-specific knowledge and skills tests.

title: X Endorsement (X endorsement)
description: Combined CDL endorsement (X) covering both hazmat (H) and tanker (N); required for hauling liquid or gaseous hazardous materials.

title: CDL Class B (CDL-B)
description: Commercial Driver's License Class B — authorizes operation of single vehicles over 26,001 lbs GVWR, including straight trucks, dump trucks, and most buses.

title: CDL Class C (CDL-C)
description: Commercial Driver's License Class C — for single vehicles under 26,001 lbs GVWR transporting 16+ passengers or placardable hazmat.

title: HOS 11-Hour Driving Rule
description: FMCSA Hours-of-Service rule limiting commercial drivers to 11 hours of driving within a 14-hour on-duty window; requires 10 hours off-duty before reset.

title: HOS 14-Hour Window
description: FMCSA Hours-of-Service rule defining the 14-hour on-duty window during which 11 hours of driving and required breaks must occur.

title: HOS 30-Minute Break
description: FMCSA Hours-of-Service rule requiring a 30-minute break after 8 cumulative driving hours; break can be off-duty, on-duty not driving, or sleeper berth.

title: HOS 70-Hour Rule (8-Day)
description: FMCSA Hours-of-Service rule limiting commercial drivers to 70 hours of on-duty time in any 8 consecutive days; resets via 34-hour rest.

title: HOS 34-Hour Restart
description: Optional FMCSA Hours-of-Service provision allowing a 34-hour off-duty period to reset the 8-day (70-hour) cumulative on-duty clock.

title: Settlement Statement
description: Weekly statement from a carrier to a lease-on owner-operator (or 1099 contractor) detailing gross revenue, deductions, and net pay.

title: Escrow Deductions
description: Weekly deductions held by a carrier in a reserve account, typically funding maintenance, repair, or end-of-lease balloon; refundable on terms set in the lease.

title: Fuel Advance
description: Cash advance from a carrier or factoring company against the operator's next pay or invoice, specifically for fuel purchases; typically 25-50% of expected pay.

title: Maintenance Escrow
description: Specific escrow category covering anticipated maintenance and repair costs; typically $100-$250/week deducted by carriers from lease-on operators' pay.

title: Bobtail Pay
description: Compensation paid to a driver for moving the tractor without a trailer (bobtail movement); covers fuel and time for repositioning runs.

title: Detention Pay Policy
description: Carrier or broker-specific policy defining when, how, and how much detention pay is owed; varies widely from formal contracts to ad hoc claims.

title: Guaranteed Pay
description: Carrier compensation structure ensuring a minimum weekly amount regardless of actual miles driven; common in dedicated and high-priority operations.

title: Safety Bonus
description: Carrier-paid bonus tied to driver safety metrics (CSA improvements, accident-free quarters, telematics scorecard); typically $500-$3,000/quarter.

title: Referral Bonus
description: Carrier-paid bonus for referring qualified drivers; typically $500-$5,000 paid after the referred driver completes a probationary period.

title: Orientation Pay
description: Compensation paid to a new driver during the carrier orientation period (typically 2-7 days), covering training, paperwork, and DOT drug testing.

title: Trip Sheet
description: Driver-maintained record of load details, fuel purchases, miles driven by state, and expenses for a single trip; supports IFTA and tax reporting.

title: Carrier Deduction
description: Any deduction taken by a motor carrier from a lease-on owner-operator's settlement; includes fuel, escrow, insurance, dispatch, communications, and miscellaneous.

title: Dispatched blog — trucking finance & operations
description: Long-form posts on owner-operator economics, factoring, equipment financing, and trucking operations.

title: How to calculate true cost per mile for your trucking operation
description: Stop guessing. Calculate your real cost per mile in 8 steps, including fuel, fixed costs, factoring, depreciation, and deadhead. Plus the diagnostic number that tells you if you're profitable.

title: What new owner-operators get wrong about commercial trucking insurance
description: Insurance is 4-8% of revenue for owner-operators — and most new operators get it wrong. Coverage gaps, broker assumptions, premium financing traps, and CSA score impact.

title: Lease-purchase vs financing: the math the carrier doesn't show you
description: Lease-purchase programs sell easy entry. 80%+ fail. Here's the actual math comparing total cost, equity build, and the failure-rate-adjusted expected value.

title: Picking your first factoring company: a decision framework
description: Stop comparing rates alone. The 6-factor framework for picking the right factor: contract terms, advance rate, fuel program, recourse structure, customer service tier, and growth fit.

title: How to read a freight broker rate confirmation
description: Rate cons hide details that matter. Detention windows, fuel surcharges, accessorial pay, lumper reimbursement, and the clauses that decide if a load is profitable.

title: What to do when your MC# is deactivated
description: MC# deactivation stops your revenue overnight. The five causes, the recovery process, and how to avoid it. Plus how factoring and lending react to the deactivation event.

title: The hidden costs of accepting low-rate spot freight
description: Accepting a $1.80/mile load to fill the truck looks like revenue. The math says it's a loss. Operating costs, deadhead, opportunity cost, and the rate floor every operator should know.

title: Building business credit as an owner-operator
description: Business credit takes 18-24 months to build properly. The 6-step path from MC# activation to a real Paydex score and the SBA loan that unlocks. Practical playbook.

title: Understanding CSA scores: what they actually mean for your operation
description: CSA percentile scores affect insurance, broker access, and lender underwriting. The 7 BASICs, how to improve them, and what the 24-month rolling window means.

title: Factoring vs working capital: when to use which
description: Factoring accelerates broker payments. Working capital loans bridge cash flow. Which one to use, when to use both, and when to use neither.

title: How much down payment do you actually need on a truck?
description: 10%, 15%, 20% down? Zero down? The right down payment depends on credit, revenue history, and truck age. Plus the trade-off between lower monthly and total interest cost.

title: IRP and IFTA: the survival guide
description: IRP base state selection and IFTA quarterly reporting trip up new owner-operators every year. The mechanics, audit defense, and how ELD data simplifies both.

title: Dispatch services vs self-dispatch: the trade-off
description: 5-10% of gross goes to dispatch services. Self-dispatch saves the fee but costs time. When dispatch is worth it, when it isn't, and the math that decides.

title: How much should you budget for truck maintenance?
description: Maintenance is 15-25 cents per mile for OTR Class 8. The cost categories, when to budget more, and the maintenance-fund approach that prevents emergency MCAs.

title: Motor truck cargo insurance: what you actually need
description: $100K minimum is the default but rarely the right answer. Coverage limits by freight type, what's excluded, claim filing mechanics, and reefer breakdown gotchas.

title: How to vet a freight broker before booking the load
description: Most broker disputes start with brokers you shouldn't have booked with. The pre-booking checks, MC# verification, factoring-company broker credit, and red flags.

title: Insurance claim filing for owner-operators: a step-by-step playbook
description: When a claim happens, the next 48 hours matter most. The 7-step filing process, what to document, when to involve an attorney, and the mistakes that hurt your premium.

title: Factoring contract walk-through: what every line actually means
description: Most factoring contracts use the same boilerplate. The 12 sections that matter, the clauses that trap operators, and the negotiation language that gets you better terms.

title: The 90-day CSA improvement playbook
description: CSA percentiles drive insurance, broker access, and lender risk. The 90-day plan to improve your scores, starting with the BASIC most likely to move first.

title: Roadside inspection survival guide for owner-operators
description: The 5 inspection levels, the documents to keep accessible, the common violations that compound into CSA points, and how to handle an inspector professionally.

title: The new-authority Year 1 compliance calendar
description: Day 1, Month 1, Month 6, Year 1. The compliance milestones every new MC# operator must hit. Filing deadlines, renewals, and the things that trip up first-year carriers.

title: Equipment buyback offers: when to take them and when to walk
description: Dealer or carrier buyback offers feel like an easy exit. The math reveals when they're fair and when they're undercutting your equity. The framework, plus 3 worked examples.

title: Working capital vs factoring vs MCA: the decision matrix
description: Three cash-flow tools, three completely different products. The decision matrix that tells you which to use when, and the trap of using all three simultaneously.

title: Refinancing your truck loan: when it actually saves money
description: Refinancing can save thousands or cost thousands. The math, the FICO improvement timing, and the prepayment penalty trap most operators don't see coming.

title: SBA loans for owner-operators: eligibility, timeline, and the trade-off
description: SBA loans offer the lowest commercial rates available — but the 60-90 day timeline and paperwork burden disqualify them for emergency needs. The eligibility, the math, the path.

title: Reading your settlement statement: what every line means
description: Your weekly settlement statement has 30+ line items. The categories that matter, the deductions that get miscoded, and the math that reveals whether your carrier is treating you fairly.

title: Nuclear verdict defense: what your dash cam needs to capture
description: Trucking nuclear verdicts (over $10M) grew 800%+ since 2014. The dash cam evidence that defends carriers, the 7 angles to capture, and the storage rules that protect the evidence.

title: Cargo claim filing: the playbook that protects your factoring relationship
description: Cargo claims hit your motor truck cargo policy AND your factoring relationship. The 6-step filing process, settlement math, and how to avoid the chargeback trap.

title: Premium financing math: when monthly payments beat paying cash
description: Premium financing spreads annual insurance into monthly payments at 8-15% APR. The math comparing premium financing vs cash pay, plus the cancellation risk most operators don't model.

title: Bobtail and non-trucking liability decoded for lease-on operators
description: Lease-on operators have coverage gaps the carrier's primary doesn't fill. NTL/bobtail coverage, what it does and doesn't cover, and the $400/year decision that protects $1M of exposure.

title: Hot-shot vs Class 8 insurance pricing — what actually drives the difference
description: Hot-shot insurance can be 30-50% cheaper than Class 8. Or it can be more expensive. The variables that drive the pricing gap, and the underwriting logic behind it.

title: Trucking lender directory
description: Editorial directory of trucking lenders — factoring, equipment, working capital, ABL, and insurance-premium financing.

title: Apex Capital
description: Three decades of owner-operator factoring with instant 24/7/365 funding via blynk® and the deepest fuel discount in the market (operator-reported ~51¢/gal at participating chains). 12-month auto-renewing contract with a 30-day cancellation window is the main trade-off.

title: eCapital
description: Largest North American freight factor by volume with deep mid-fleet and broker products plus ABL and equipment financing under one roof. Fee transparency and contract-exit complaints are the most common review themes — read the schedule in writing before signing.

title: RTS Financial
description: Trucking-native factor with a solid fuel-card network and broker-credit tool sized for 10–50 truck fleets. Does not edge out Apex on owner-operators or eCapital on the largest fleets, but a fair quote-against for mid-sized operators.

title: Triumph Business Capital
description: Strongest non-recourse program on the list — built around broker-credit risk as a core product, not a bolt-on. Owned by publicly traded Triumph Financial, which keeps pricing disciplined but rigid; the 12-month contract auto-renews.

title: TBS Factoring
description: Funds brand-new MC numbers in their first 30 days where most factors require 90+ days of broker history. Supportive of post-bankruptcy operators and includes a fuel card plus dispatch support; customer service is functional rather than high-touch.

title: OTR Solutions
description: No minimum volume and no minimum contract — spot factoring on the loads you choose with everything else in-house. Per-invoice rate (2.5–5%) is higher than full-ledger factoring, so the math only works if you factor selectively.

title: Porter Freight Funding
description: Explicit no-personal-credit-check option for sub-580 FICOs, recent bankruptcies, and thin-file operators — they underwrite the broker, not the trucker. No fuel program and a 90% advance cap raise the effective cost vs. Apex or eCapital.

title: Truckstop Go Capital
description: Native factoring product inside the Truckstop load board — invoices flow from booked loads into the factoring queue without re-entry. Higher headline rate (3.25%+) than Apex or Triumph; the integration value disappears if you do not run loads through Truckstop.

title: 1st Commercial Credit
description: Broad SMB factoring desk with a trucking-specific team and open underwriting for operators other factors decline. Trade-off: not trucking-specialized in the way Apex, TBS, and Triumph are — the fuel program and broker-credit tooling are thinner.

title: Riviera Finance
description: Legacy factor since 1969 with a national branch network — the only major factor on this list with in-person branch relationships. Technology stack (instant payment, mobile capture) is dated relative to Apex, eCapital, and Truckstop Go.

title: Bluevine
description: Tech-forward online lender offering revolving lines of credit up to $250K with same-day decisioning for clean files. Not trucking-native — works for owner-operators with strong personal credit and clean bank statements, but the underwriting does not flex for thin-file or new-authority profiles.

title: Lendio
description: Lender marketplace that shops a single application across 75+ partner lenders for term loans, lines of credit, and equipment financing. Convenient for first-pass shopping; the downside is generic SMB underwriting rather than trucking-specific products from any single desk.

description: Estimate a realistic factoring advance rate and fee by authority age, monthly volume, and broker concentration.

description: Compute the dollar cost of factoring against a target advance rate, fee tier, and monthly invoice volume.

description: Compare leasing vs buying a tractor with realistic depreciation, payment, and tax-deduction assumptions.

description: Estimate the payment, term, and rate range on a truck-repair loan based on your FICO band and repair amount.

description: Estimate the monthly payment and term on a Class 8 tractor loan by truck price, down payment, and credit band.
